failure_modes.text: Low-competence actors can overestimate their ability because they lack the skill required to notice their own mistakes.
failure_modes.source_quote: Individuals with limited knowledge or low competence tend to significantly overestimate their abilities.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use objective tests and calibration exercises rather than self-assessment; low-competence actors lack the skill to notice their own mistakes.

failure_modes.text: Organizations can mistake fluency, confidence, or slight knowledge advantage for real competence and give too much trust too early.
failure_modes.source_quote: Demonstrating slightly more knowledge can cause the audience to assume that person is a guru.
failure_modes.mitigation: Verify competence through demonstrated performance in realistic conditions, not through confidence, fluency, or slight knowledge advantage.

failure_modes.text: Dunning-Kruger becomes a lazy insult when the label replaces calibration, coaching, objective tests, or evidence-bearing review.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the Dunning-Kruger label is used as a lazy dismissal of someone else's view.
failure_modes.mitigation: Replace the Dunning-Kruger label with concrete calibration data: coaching, evidence-bearing review, and skill tests are more useful than insults.

premortem_questions.text: What objective test, external review, or observable performance would falsify this person's self-assessment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Self-assessment can be compared to objective tests, external review, or observed performance instead of being taken at face value.

premortem_questions.text: Are we trusting confidence and fluency, or are we actually checking operating skill under real conditions?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Teams need to separate apparent fluency or charisma from real operating skill.

premortem_questions.text: How might status, experience, or prior success be creating the same illusion of understanding in our experts?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger when experts assume the bias belongs only to novices.

heuristics.text: Never trust self-assessment alone in a high-consequence domain.
heuristics.source_quote: The DKE is powerful in contexts where self-assessment by novices is often useless.

heuristics.text: Use calibration, external review, and performance evidence to separate real competence from overconfidence.
heuristics.source_quote: Calibration: regularly use objective instruments like quizzes, practice tests, or external review processes.

heuristics.text: Treat Dunning-Kruger as a prompt for better evidence and coaching, not as a shortcut for dismissing people.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger when the Dunning-Kruger label is used as a lazy dismissal.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the chunks on overconfidence, skill calibration, and anti-label misuse discipline.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broader personality or impostor-syndrome sprawl beyond the clearest calibration moves.
open_questions: The source supports coaching nuance around experts and novices, but the Wave 2 surface stays on competence calibration.

allies: The raw source is strongest alongside confidence-calibration, intellectual-humility, and Feynman-technique style blind-spot exposure.
antagonists: The strongest conflicts are with overconfidence, guru projection, and inside-view overtrust.
structured_tensions: The main tensions concern novice overconfidence, expert illusion of understanding, and lazy label misuse.
